title: The Rise of Edge Computing
excerpt: Why the future of the web is moving closer to the user.
content: Edge computing is fundamentally changing how we deploy applications by moving logic away from centralized data centers.
By processing data closer to the source, we can achieve near-instantaneous load times.
This shift is particularly impactful for IoT devices and real-time tools.

title: Productivity Hacks for Remote Devs
excerpt: Simple habits to keep your focus sharp while working from home.
content: Working from home offers great flexibility, but it also introduces unique distractions.
Establishing a dedicated workspace helps the brain transition between professional and personal time.
We recommend the Pomodoro technique combined with proactive calendar blocking.

title: Understanding CSS Grid
excerpt: A beginner's guide to the most powerful layout system in CSS.
content: CSS Grid is a two-dimensional layout system for aligning elements into rows and columns.
Unlike Flexbox, Grid gives you total control over both axes simultaneously.
Modern browsers now have excellent support for Grid layouts.

title: Draft: Q3 Roadmap Planning
excerpt: Internal notes on upcoming features and infrastructure upgrades.
content: Our primary focus will be on migrating our legacy database.
We also plan to introduce a new plugin architecture for third-party developers.
Final approval is expected during the executive review next month.

title: The Importance of Unit Testing
excerpt: How writing tests today saves you hundreds of hours of debugging tomorrow.
content: Unit testing is the most effective way to document code behavior.
A robust test suite acts as a safety net for refactoring logic.
Ideally, tests should be small, isolated, and fast.

title: State Management in 2026
excerpt: Moving beyond Redux: Why signals and atomic state are taking over.
content: The landscape of state management has shifted away from heavy, centralized stores toward more granular, reactive primitives.
Signals allow for fine-grained updates, ensuring that only the specific components needing data are re-rendered.
This approach reduces boilerplate significantly and makes complex application logic much easier to track and debug.

title: WebAssembly for Frontend Devs
excerpt: How to run high-performance C++ and Rust code directly in the browser.
content: WebAssembly (Wasm) is no longer a niche technology for game engines; it is becoming a staple for heavy data processing in web apps.
By compiling languages like Rust to Wasm, we can perform complex image manipulation and physics simulations at native speeds.
Integration with JavaScript has become seamless, allowing developers to use Wasm modules just like standard ES modules.

title: The Evolution of Type Safety
excerpt: Why TypeScript is just the beginning of the type-safe web revolution.
content: Type safety has moved from being a luxury to a requirement for modern enterprise-grade web development.
We are seeing a rise in 'end-to-end' type safety, where database schemas automatically generate frontend types.
This eliminates a whole class of runtime errors that previously plagued large-scale JavaScript applications.

title: Draft: Performance Audit Results
excerpt: Preliminary findings from our Q2 site speed investigation.
content: The latest Lighthouse scores show a 15% regression in Largest Contentful Paint (LCP) on our checkout page.
This appears to be caused by a new third-party tracking script that blocks the main thread during hydration.
Our next steps include moving these scripts to a Web Worker to free up the UI thread for user interactions.

title: Building Accessible Components
excerpt: Practical tips for ensuring your UI is usable by everyone.
content: Accessibility is not just a checklist; it is about creating an inclusive experience for users with varying abilities.
Using semantic HTML elements like <main>, <nav>, and <button> provides out-of-the-box support for screen readers.
Always ensure that your interactive elements have clear focus states and meet minimum color contrast requirements.
